context: For olde payens° that idoles heried°
After his sone,° and after riche thinges
Of gentil° wommen, tender creatures
That love with false loveres werketh° so;
Save° wilde bestes, and that ful many oon,°
Lat maidens gon to daunce, and yonge wives.”
And been of good condiciouns therto;
A Greek espie, and telleth newe thinges
And empten° also grete and hevy purses

context: And telle of false men that hem bitrayen,°
Thus writen olde clerkes in hir lives.
By sadde° tokenes° and by wordes bolde,°
And smale titheres° weren foule yshent.°
Mak ye yow newe bodies thus alway
With dede bodies in ful sondry wise,°
I trowe° he hadde wives mo than oon;
And ech of hem had wives mo than two;
For I shal telle ensamples mo than ten.
He knew of hem mo legendes and lives
And therwithal, he knew of mo proverbes
“Now, Cristes owene knightes leve° and dere
Rehersinge of° thise olde poetries,°
Of queinte° mirours and of prospectives,°
And deintees° mo than been in my knowing.
In which were peinted al thise false foules,°
That never yet was herd so° grete mervailles.
A thousand olde stories thee alegge
That after sharpe shoures been victories.
And, to deliveren him from bittre bondes
Yet were me lever° houndes had me eten
Of scole-termes;° wiser men than thow
Of spices hote,° t’encresen his corage;°
The which° that he had knowe in olde dawes;°
And som with arwes blede of° bittre woundes.
Now lakketh us no tales mo than oon.
A thousand sikes, hottere than the glede
The swifte Fame, which that false thinges
Was thorughout Troye yfled with preste winges
And with hir tales, dere ynough a mite
Withouten wordes mo, they goon to bedde
Of thewes° gode, yset in heigh bountee,°
¶Whilom,° as olde stories tellen us
So wel they loved, as olde bokes seyn
Of fine rubies and of diamaunts.
What ladies fairest been or best daunsinge
As doon thise wete brondes° in hir brenninge,°
The sheeldes brighte, testers,° and trappures;°
Of olde folk, and folk of tendre yeres
In clothes blake, ydropped al with teres;
I mene, he speke wol of smale thinges
For in the sterres, clerer than is glas,°
And deintees° mo than I can yow devise
Of olde Britons, dwellinge in this ile;
May winne of me mo fees° thus
Upon my bed with brighte bemes
To have mo floures, swiche seven°
Wher° ther were many grene greves,°
In alle thinges moor mesure
Right faire shuldres, and body longe°
Right white handes, and nailes rede
Rounde brestes; and of good brede°
Good men and wommen, o° thing warne I yow
Of olde stories, longe time agoon:
¶Wel worthe,° of this thing, grete clerkes
And mo riche tabernacles°
And with perre° mo pinacles
In stede of reste and newe thinges
And mo loves new begonne
And mo loves casuelly°
Mo discords, mo jelousies
Mo discords, mo jelousies
Mo murmurs, and mo novelries
Mo murmurs, and mo novelries
And mo dissimulacions;
And mo berdes° in two houres
And also mo renovelaunces°
Mo love-dayes° and acordes°
And now, unethes,° grete bestes;
As flakes falle in grete snowes.
That maden loude menstralcies
Reyes,° and these straunge thinges.
Olde wicches, sorceresses
As wel as other olde mervailes
Of hem that writen olde gestes,°
Good werkes shal yow noght availe°
Han these sory creatures!
Of faire windes, and of tempestes
Wher thou maist most tidinges here;
“Wel worth of dremes ay thise olde wives
Thou most a fewe of olde stories here
Hath lordes olde; through which, withinne a throwe
“Your lettres ful, the papir al ypleinted
Lo here, of payens corsed olde rites
And over the gate, with lettres large ywroghte,°
“There been mo sterres,° God wot, than a paire!”
Fetis° and smale, and yonge fruitesteres,°
I make avow to Goddes digne° bones!
¶Now, good men, God foryeve yow your trespas,°
With wilde mares, as faste as he may go.
His nailes whitter than the lilie flour
Of arwes,° and of fir with rede lemes,°
For fere of blake beres, or boles° blake
Or elles, blake develes wole hem take.
As seith my lord, so make us al good men;
Thus often wise men ben war by folis.
Ensample why, see now these wise clerkes
In hote coles he hath himselven raked,°
This king of kinges proud was and elaat,°
And heriest° false goddes cursedly;
And she that helmed° was in starke° stoures,°
That regnes° mo putte in subjeccioun
How that to thise grete conqueroures two
